one_line_takeaway: SecCoderX trains a CWE-conditioned reasoning reward model from repurposed vulnerability-detection datasets and uses it in an online GRPO loop with length/AST-similarity functionality proxies, closing the 'functionality-security paradox' seen in prior secure-code alignment baselines (which degraded Effective Safety Rate by 14-54%) by instead improving ESR by roughly 10% over the unaligned base model across three code LLMs.
key_finding: On the secure code generation benchmarks (Table 2, averaged across CyberSecEval SCG and CWEval), SecCoderX improves ESR by roughly 10% relative to the corresponding unaligned base model across all three target models (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder-7B: base ESR 32.45 -> SecCoderX 35.82; Qwen2.5-Coder-3B: base 28.44 -> SecCoderX 31.91; CodeLlama-7B: base 19.96 -> SecCoderX 21.94), while the two baselines degrade ESR sharply relative to base in every case (e.g., CodeLlama-7B: ProSec drops to 11.60, SafeCoder to 17.15, both well below the base's 19.96; the paper states prior methods degrade ESR by 14-54% overall). SecCoderX also raises raw Safety rate by 11-16 percentage points over the unaligned model while largely preserving HumanEval+/MBPP+ pass@1/pass@10 performance without adding extra general-coding instruction-tuning data (unlike SafeCoder's use of Code Evol-Instruct or ProSec's use of Infinity-Instruct). The 8B CWE-conditioned reward model achieves the best or second-best F1 among all compared detectors (Table 1: average F1 67.90, versus GPT-4.1's 66.34, Gemini-2.5-Flash's 65.09, and R2Vul-7B's 66.68), despite being smaller than the closed-source comparators. Ablations (Table 3) show reasoning-based SFT is necessary (plain SFT without reasoning traces actually degrades F1 below the untuned Qwen3-8B base, from 57.71 to 53.75, while reasoning-SFT raises it to 62.61), GRPO further improves recall/F1 to 67.90, and CWE-conditioning contributes an additional gain over an otherwise-identical RM (63.98 -> 67.90 F1). Ablations on the RL reward (Table 4) show the vulnerability reward is the primary driver of Safety/ESR (removing it costs -5.95 Safety, -2.61 ESR), the length reward regularizes exploration (removing it costs -5.73 Safety), and the AST reward is what anchors functionality (removing it costs -5.55 Func despite a +1.97 Safety uptick), consistent with the paper's claim that the interaction reward term couples security and functionality rather than trading one for the other.
core_contribution: SecCoderX is an online reinforcement-learning (GRPO) framework for aligning code LLMs to generate code that is simultaneously secure and functionally correct, targeting a phenomenon the authors call the 'functionality-security paradox' where prior secure-code alignment methods raise raw safety rates but wreck functional correctness. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a two-stage pipeline that repurposes existing vulnerability-detection datasets (PrimeVul, R2Vul) into 'reality-grounded' vulnerability-inducing coding task prompts, by first using Gemini-2.5-Pro to infer N plausible repository contexts for a vulnerable snippet's logic and then generating a coding-task instruction conditioned on each (repository, CWE, language) tuple -- yielding a released dataset of 24k prompts spanning 24 CWE categories and 5 programming languages; (2) a CWE-conditioned, reasoning-based 8B vulnerability reward model (built by SFT'ing Qwen3-8B on GPT-4.1-distilled reasoning traces over PrimeVul/DiverseVul, then further trained with GRPO on R2Vul for generalization), motivated by the authors' argument that SAST tools are unsuitable as an online-RL reward source (limited CWE coverage, high latency such as CodeQL, and a requirement for fully compilable code that function-level rollouts don't satisfy); and (3) a composite GRPO reward -- format reward + binary vulnerability reward (from the RM) + a length-deviation reward (thresholds alpha=+50%, beta=-30%, sigma=-50% on line-count change vs. a pre-alignment reference generation) + an AST-similarity reward, combined with an explicit interaction term r_interact = r_vul * [r_len(1+r_ast)] that only rewards secure generations when they also preserve the reference code's structure, to jointly optimize security and functionality preservation.
threat_model: The paper does not model an adversary attacking a deployed system; the 'threat' is the code LLM itself generating insecure code under ordinary (non-adversarial) coding prompts. Security is operationalized purely via the CWE taxonomy: generated code y is 'secure' if it exhibits no known exploit pattern associated with a defined CWE category (Section 2, Task Formulation, Eq. 2: minimize Pr[y in Y_CWE] under natural prompts x ~ P). There is no adaptive/adversarial-prompt threat model in the main alignment objective -- vulnerability-inducing prompts are synthesized offline for training rollouts, not adversarially crafted at inference time against the aligned model.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implicit limitations: (1) Functionality is never verified against ground-truth unit-test execution during training -- the RL functionality signal is a proxy (line-length deviation + AST structural similarity to a single pre-alignment reference generation), which the authors justify by cost but which could reward structurally similar-yet-still-broken code, or penalize legitimate large restructurings needed for a genuine fix. (2) The vulnerability reward model is explicitly CWE-conditioned on the target CWE that was baked into each synthesized prompt during data generation -- the paper states this 'transforms vulnerability detection from an open-ended search problem into targeted verification of the intended CWE classes,' meaning the RM's strong detection numbers in Table 1 (and its role as an RL reward) benefit from knowing which vulnerability class to check for, a much easier task than open-ended vulnerability discovery; generalization to unknown/unlabeled CWE categories at deployment is not evaluated. (3) Absolute Safety/Func/ESR numbers remain modest even for SecCoderX (e.g., best ESR 35.82% for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B), i.e., most generations are still not simultaneously secure-and-functional; the paper frames its contribution as reducing the ESR degradation of prior alignment methods, not as achieving high absolute security. (4) The synthesized 24k training prompts are themselves LLM-generated (via Gemini-2.5-Pro repository inference and prompt synthesis) rather than sourced from real developer requests, so realism of the RL training distribution relative to actual production coding prompts is asserted but not independently validated against real-world prompt distributions. (5) Evaluated only on three relatively small-to-mid-size open code models (CodeLlama-7B, Qwen2.5-Coder-3B/7B); scaling behavior to larger models is not demonstrated in the main results (a closed/larger-model comparison is deferred to an appendix, Section F.1, not extracted here).

datasets_used: PrimeVul (Ding et al., 2024) -- seed vulnerability-detection dataset for task synthesis and RM training
R2Vul (Weyssow et al., 2025b) -- seed dataset for task synthesis; also used for RM GRPO stage and as an RM evaluation benchmark
CrossVul (Nikitopoulos et al., 2021) -- vulnerability-detection data mixed into RM training set
DiverseVul (Chen et al., 2023) -- used for distilling reasoning traces for RM SFT
SecCoderX's own released dataset: 24k vulnerability-inducing coding prompts spanning 24 CWE categories and 5 programming languages (C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Python)
CyberSecEval SCG (Bhatt et al., 2023) -- secure code generation evaluation benchmark
CWEval (Peng et al., 2025) -- secure code generation evaluation benchmark, source of the ESR metric
SVEN (He & Vechev, 2023) and ProSec (Xu et al., 2024) benchmarks -- used to evaluate the vulnerability reward model's detection performance
HumanEval+ (Chen, 2021) and MBPP+ (Austin et al., 2021) -- general coding capability benchmarks
quantitative_results: Table 2, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B: Base ESR 32.45 -> SecCoderX ESR 35.82; Base Safety 61.53 -> SecCoderX Safety 69.40; ProSec* ESR 20.53, SafeCoder ESR 20.15
Table 2, Qwen2.5-Coder-3B: Base ESR 28.44 -> SecCoderX ESR 31.91; ProSec ESR 13.08, SafeCoder ESR 19.49
Table 2, CodeLlama-7B: Base ESR 19.96 -> SecCoderX ESR 21.94; ProSec ESR 11.60, SafeCoder ESR 17.15
Table 1, Reward model average F1 across PrimeVul/SVEN/ProSec/R2Vul: SecCoderX RM 8B = 67.90, vs GPT-4.1 = 66.34, Gemini-2.5-Flash = 65.09, R2Vul-7B = 66.68, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Inst = 17.10
Table 3, RM ablation average F1: Base(Qwen3-8B) 57.71 -> w/o Reasoning SFT 53.75 -> with Reasoning SFT 62.61 -> Full w/o CWE-Cond 63.98 -> Full (SecCoderX RM) 67.90

Table 4, RL reward ablation (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, avg over secure benchmarks): Full Safety 54.03/Func 56.31/ESR 35.82; w/o Vulnerability reward: Safety -5.95, Func +3.38, ESR -2.61; w/o Length reward: Safety -5.73, Func +0.82, ESR -2.30; w/o AST Matching: Safety +1.97, Func -5.55, ESR +0.54; w/o Format: Safety +1.39, Func -1.67, ESR +0.61

Paper-level summary claims: SecCoderX improves ESR by approximately 10% over unaligned models; prior methods (SafeCoder, ProSec) degrade ESR by 14-54%; SecCoderX raises Safety rate by 11-16 percentage points over unaligned models
baselines_compared: SafeCoder (He et al., 2024) -- supervised instruction-tuning-based secure code alignment baseline
ProSec (Xu et al., 2024) -- preference-optimization (DPO-style) secure code alignment baseline; a reproduced variant (ProSec*) used for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B
GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Flash -- closed-source LLM baselines for vulnerability-detection/reward-model comparison
R2Vul-7B, Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-14B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B/14B-Instruct -- open-source detection baselines in Table 1
Base (unaligned) versions of CodeLlama-7B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct -- the pre-alignment reference models
